title: Combinatorial Inequalities and Combinatorial Interpretations: Part III
speaker: Igor Pak
abstract:
In the third talk, I will concentrate on inequalities for linear extensionsof finite posets.  I will start with several inequalities which do have a combinatorial proof.  I will then turn to Stanley's inequality and outline the proof why its defect does not have a combinatorialinterpretation.  Time permitting I will discuss what does that sayabout the equality cases and stability of the Alexandrov-Fenchelinequality.
Joint work with Swee Hong Chan (STOC 2024).

proof_stages.label: Linear extensions and counting background
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines finite posets, linear extensions, and the count e(P), then motivates the subject by computable families, hardness of exact counting, and randomized approximation.

proof_stages.label: Stanley position-sequence log-concavity
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed element x, the numbers N(a) of linear extensions with f(x)=a are defined, Stanley's log-concavity inequality N(a)^2 >= N(a-1)N(a+1) is stated, and the defect Delta(a) is singled out.

proof_stages.label: Order-polytope volume model
proof_stages.summary: The order polytope O(P) is introduced and Stanley's volume lemma vol O(P)=e(P)/n! is proved by triangulating O(P) into one simplex for each linear extension.

proof_stages.label: Alexandrov-Fenchel proof by slices
proof_stages.summary: Alexandrov-Fenchel mixed-volume log-concavity is recalled, and slices of the order polytope at g(x)=0 and g(x)=1 are used to realize the N(a)'s as mixed volumes, proving Stanley's inequality.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial alternatives and weighted strengthening
proof_stages.summary: The order-polynomial analogue with a direct injection is contrasted with linear extensions, and a weighted log-concavity theorem for linear extensions is stated as a stronger result proved by combinatorial-atlas methods rather than Alexandrov-Fenchel.

proof_stages.label: Pinned-position inequalities and equality complexity
proof_stages.summary: Stanley's stronger inequality with additional elements pinned to specified ranks is formulated, and the talk states complexity results showing polynomial-time equality tests in the k=0,1 cases but polynomial-hierarchy hardness phenomena once at least two additional ranks are fixed.

proof_stages.label: Relative ratios and attainable height-two counts
proof_stages.summary: A relative extension-count ratio rho(P,x)=e(P)/e(P-x) is introduced as a reduction gadget, leading to a final conjecture that all sufficiently large integers occur as linear-extension counts of height-two posets, together with computational evidence and variants.

examples.name: Constant-q weight specialization
examples.note: Taking the order-reversing weight function omega to be constantly q makes the weighted statistic a simple q-power, clarifying the general weighted theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the weight function to a constant q and traces that the weight becomes q to the size of the relevant down-set, with q=1 recovering Stanley’s theorem

passes.reason: Setting all weights equal to q collapses the general weight data to a uniform invariant specialization.
